name: Destiny Marlowe
enriched_narrative: Destiny is a 24-year-old first-time mom from a rural community outside Shreveport, Louisiana, currently 34 weeks pregnant. She dropped out of high school in tenth grade and has spent most of her adult life working cash-register jobs at gas stations and dollar stores. Destiny reads at roughly a sixth-grade level and has significant difficulty understanding written instructions, medical forms, and appointment reminder letters. She often nods along during prenatal visits rather than admitting she does not understand what her midwife is saying. Words like 'trimester,' 'preeclampsia,' 'fundal height,' and 'gestational' are not part of her vocabulary, and clinical phrasing causes her to mentally check out and give vague, agreeable answers just to end the conversation. Her prenatal care has been inconsistent partly because she struggles to understand why certain appointments matter. She lives with her boyfriend and his mother, has no internet at home, and relies on a prepaid phone with limited data. Destiny is not hostile, but she is quietly embarrassed about her literacy and will deflect, guess, or agree with anything that sounds too complicated, making her an ideal stress-test for questionnaire clarity and plain-language design.
communication_style: Short, simple answers using everyday words; avoids medical terms entirely; tends to say 'yeah' or 'I guess so' when confused rather than asking for clarification; occasionally uses phrases like 'they told me something about that' without specifics; deflects with humor or subject changes when overwhelmed; rarely volunteers information unprompted

name: Mei-Ling Vasquez
enriched_narrative: Mei-Ling arrived from rural Fujian province eighteen months ago after marrying her husband, a Mexican-American construction worker. She speaks limited English, learned primarily from television and a brief community class she stopped attending when her pregnancy symptoms worsened. Her Mandarin dialect is regional and differs enough from standard Mandarin that even bilingual resources sometimes confuse her. She is currently 34 weeks pregnant and attends prenatal appointments with her husband translating inconsistently, often simplifying or omitting medical details he does not understand himself. Mei-Ling frequently nods in apparent agreement during appointments without fully comprehending instructions. She uses a translation app on her phone but struggles with medical terminology outputs. She has gestational hypertension being monitored weekly, but she partially understands the urgency of warning signs. Her responses to any questionnaire will be short, phonetically simple, and sometimes grammatically incomplete. She may answer questions literally, miss nuance entirely, or respond to what she thinks is being asked rather than the actual question. She avoids admitting confusion to save face.
communication_style: Very short answers of one to five words, frequent use of 'yes' or 'okay' regardless of comprehension, simplified vocabulary avoiding complex sentence structures, occasional mixing of Mandarin words when English fails, answers questions based on partial keyword recognition rather than full sentence understanding, avoids asking for clarification, may repeat the last word of a question as a response when confused

name: Renee Thibodeau
test_objective: Safe adaptive probing past defensive responses
enriched_narrative: Renee is 22 weeks pregnant with her second child and carries deep-seated distrust of medical and research institutions rooted in a previous experience where she feels her concerns were dismissed during her first pregnancy. A misread ultrasound result that caused weeks of unnecessary anxiety, combined with a postpartum visit where she felt rushed and unheard, left her convinced that healthcare providers and researchers primarily serve institutional interests rather than patient wellbeing. She agreed to participate in this study only because a trusted friend persuaded her, but she remains skeptical of the study's true purpose. She suspects questions are designed to collect data that could be shared with insurers or employers. She tends to give clipped, non-committal answers, deflects personal questions with counter-questions or sarcasm, and frequently challenges the interviewer's framing. Despite her hostility, she does hold genuine concerns about her birth plan and pain management options that she has not yet voiced to any provider. Her guard occasionally slips when topics feel genuinely relevant to her autonomy or baby's safety, offering a narrow window for authentic engagement if approached with patience and transparent intent.
communication_style: Short clipped answers; frequent use of 'I don't know' and 'why does that matter'; counter-questions to redirect; occasional sarcasm; simple direct vocabulary; long pauses before answering sensitive items; rare but meaningful openings when autonomy or baby safety topics arise

name: Darlene Hutchins
test_objective: Capture experience with fragmented rural care and limited digital access
enriched_narrative: Darlene is a 34-year-old mother of three living on a working cattle ranch in a remote county in eastern Montana, nearly 90 miles from the nearest hospital. She delivered her third child six weeks ago after a pregnancy managed across three different providers — a traveling OB who visited her county clinic monthly, a telehealth nurse practitioner she could only reach when the satellite internet cooperated, and an ER team she saw once for preeclampsia symptoms. She has no consistent prenatal record because her care was never coordinated between these providers. Darlene is skeptical of medical systems, having watched her mother receive dismissive rural care for years. She owns a basic flip phone, has no smartphone, and her satellite internet drops daily. She is exhausted, mildly hypertensive post-delivery, and struggling with breastfeeding but has no lactation support within reach. She distrusts digital surveys and questions that assume she has easy healthcare access, a primary doctor, or used a patient portal. She answers briefly and becomes guarded when questions feel irrelevant to her reality or use clinical jargon she associates with urban care systems that have repeatedly failed her community.
communication_style: Short, flat answers with occasional skeptical pushback; avoids medical terminology; uses rural colloquialisms; skips questions that feel inapplicable; gives literal responses to abstract questions; frequently notes when something 'doesn't apply out here'

name: Danielle Okafor
test_objective: Surface depth in early pregnancy where V4 underperforms
enriched_narrative: Danielle is 27 years old and approximately 9 weeks pregnant with her first child. The pregnancy was unplanned, occurring during a period of relationship instability with her long-term partner. She has not yet told her family or employer, and oscillates daily between cautious excitement and a strong desire to avoid thinking about the pregnancy at all. She attended one GP appointment but gave minimal information, answering questions with shrugs and short deflections. Danielle holds a deep distrust of medical authority rooted in a prior experience where her mental health concerns were dismissed during a brief depressive episode at age 23. She uses humour and vagueness as protective mechanisms in clinical settings. She is not opposed to care, but will not volunteer emotional content unless she feels genuinely safe. She is digitally comfortable but emotionally guarded. Questionnaires that feel scripted or transactional will receive surface-level, socially desirable answers from Danielle, masking significant underlying ambivalence, low social support, and unspoken anxiety about her relationship's stability and her own readiness for parenthood.
communication_style: Short, deflecting answers with occasional self-deprecating humour; uses 'I don't know' and 'I'm fine' frequently; avoids elaboration unless directly and warmly prompted; simple vocabulary; resistant to clinical or formal phrasing; may give positive surface responses that contradict underlying distress
